quiz_questions.question_text: How many types of simple machines are there?

options.text: Six types of simple machines
Four types of simple machines
Three types of simple machines
Eight types of simple machines

quiz_questions.explanation: There are six types of simple machines: lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw. All complex machines are combinations of these six simple machines.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the main purpose of a simple machine?

options.text: To create energy from nothing at all
To make work easier by changing force or direction
To eliminate friction between all surfaces
To increase the total amount of work you do

quiz_questions.explanation: Simple machines make work easier by multiplying force, changing the direction of force, or increasing the distance over which force is applied. They do not create energy or reduce the total work needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which simple machine is a ramp an example of?

options.text: A pulley for lifting objects
A lever for prying objects up
An inclined plane for moving up
A wheel and axle for rolling

quiz_questions.explanation: A ramp is an inclined plane, one of the six simple machines. It allows you to move objects upward using less force over a longer distance, instead of lifting straight up with more force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A seesaw is an example of which simple machine?

options.text: A screw for holding things
A wedge for splitting items
A pulley for redirecting force
A lever with a fulcrum point

quiz_questions.explanation: A seesaw is a lever with the fulcrum (pivot point) in the middle. When one side goes down, the other goes up. Levers can multiply force or change its direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a pulley do?

options.text: It changes the direction of the applied force
It eliminates the need for any force at all
It increases the weight of the lifted object
It converts heat energy into kinetic energy

quiz_questions.explanation: A single pulley changes the direction of force (pulling down to lift up). Multiple pulleys can also multiply force, allowing you to lift heavy loads with less effort over a longer rope distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A knife blade is an example of which simple machine?

options.text: An inclined plane for going up
A wheel and axle for rolling
A wedge for cutting or splitting
A screw for holding things tight

quiz_questions.explanation: A knife blade is a wedge. It has a thin, angled edge that concentrates force into a small area, making it effective at cutting or splitting materials apart.

quiz_questions.question_text: A screw is actually a modified version of which other simple machine?

options.text: A lever wrapped around a cylinder
A pulley wound in a spiral shape
An inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder
A wheel and axle stacked vertically

quiz_questions.explanation: A screw is an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder. The threads of the screw are the ramp. Turning the screw moves it forward with great force over a short distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A steering wheel is an example of which simple machine?

options.text: A lever for prying objects up
A wheel and axle for rotation
A pulley for changing direction
A wedge for splitting objects

quiz_questions.explanation: A steering wheel is a wheel and axle. The large wheel allows you to apply force over a greater distance (circumference), which is converted to a larger force at the smaller axle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do simple machines reduce the total amount of work done?

options.text: Yes, they reduce total work significantly
No, they keep total work the same but change force or distance
Yes, they eliminate all work completely
No, they actually increase total work done

quiz_questions.explanation: Simple machines do not reduce total work. Work = force x distance stays the same. They trade force for distance: less force over a greater distance, or more force over a shorter distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the six simple machines?

options.text: Lever for lifting things up
Spring for storing energy here
Wedge for splitting materials
Pulley for redirecting forces

quiz_questions.explanation: A spring is not one of the six simple machines. The six are: lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a simple machine?

options.text: A basic device that changes the magnitude or direction of a force
A complex device with many moving parts like gears and motors
A machine that creates energy from nothing at all in the world
A device that only works when connected to electricity or power

quiz_questions.explanation: A simple machine is a basic mechanical device that changes the magnitude or direction of force. It has few or no moving parts and makes work easier without creating new energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a lever?

options.text: A rigid bar that rotates around a fixed pivot point
A flat surface tilted at an angle to the ground below
A rope looped over a wheel to change force direction
A thin edge used to split or cut apart materials

quiz_questions.explanation: A lever is a rigid bar that pivots around a fixed point called the fulcrum. By applying force at one end, you can move a load at the other end. Examples include seesaws, crowbars, and scissors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an inclined plane?

options.text: A flat surface set at an angle to move objects to different heights
A rope and wheel system used to lift heavy objects straight up
A thin edge designed to split or cut through hard materials
A bar that pivots around a fixed point to multiply applied force

quiz_questions.explanation: An inclined plane is a flat surface tilted at an angle. It allows you to raise objects to a higher level using less force over a longer distance. Ramps, hills, and slides are inclined planes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pulley?

options.text: A wheel with a groove for a rope that redirects force
A flat ramp used to move objects to a higher level
A sharp edge used for cutting or splitting materials
A rotating bar used to turn and move heavy objects

quiz_questions.explanation: A pulley is a wheel with a groove around it for a rope or cable. A single pulley changes the direction of force. Multiple pulleys (block and tackle) can also multiply force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a wedge?

options.text: A thick, V-shaped tool that splits or separates objects
A circular wheel used to change force direction with rope
A flat ramp tilted at an angle for moving objects up
A rod that pivots around a fulcrum point for lifting

quiz_questions.explanation: A wedge is a simple machine shaped like two inclined planes joined together. It converts a force applied to its blunt end into forces that push outward, splitting or cutting. Axes and knives are wedges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a wheel and axle?

options.text: A large wheel attached to a smaller cylinder that rotate together
A rope looped over a grooved wheel for lifting heavy objects
A rigid bar that pivots around a fixed fulcrum for leverage
A flat surface tilted at an angle for moving objects upward

quiz_questions.explanation: A wheel and axle consists of a larger wheel attached to a smaller cylinder (axle) that rotate together. Force applied to the wheel is multiplied at the axle. Doorknobs and screwdrivers are examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a screw?

options.text: An inclined plane wrapped in a spiral around a cylinder
A flat ramp placed at an angle against a wall or step
A wheel with a groove used for lifting heavy objects
A sharp edge used for cutting through hard materials

quiz_questions.explanation: A screw is an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder. The spiral threads convert rotational force into linear force. Screws can hold things together (wood screws) or lift things (jar lids, car jacks).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'effort force' mean?

options.text: The force you apply to a simple machine to do work
The weight of the object being moved by the machine
The friction that opposes the machine's movement
The energy lost as heat inside the simple machine

quiz_questions.explanation: Effort force (or input force) is the force you apply to operate a simple machine. For a lever, it is the force you push or pull with. The machine then applies a different force to the load.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'load' mean in the context of simple machines?

options.text: The object or resistance the machine is working against
The force applied by the person operating the machine
The pivot point around which the machine rotates
The material the machine itself is constructed from

quiz_questions.explanation: The load (or resistance force) is the object or force that the simple machine is working against. It is the weight being lifted, the material being cut, or the object being moved.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'mechanical advantage' mean?

options.text: How much a machine multiplies the input force applied
The speed at which a machine completes its full task
The total amount of energy a machine needs to run
The weight of the machine itself in kilograms total

quiz_questions.explanation: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of output force to input force. It tells you how much the machine multiplies your force. A mechanical advantage of 3 means the machine triples your input force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ramp is used to load a 500 N box onto a truck 1 m high. The ramp is 5 m long. Ideally, how much force is needed to push the box up the ramp?

options.text: 500 newtons of force to push up
100 newtons of force to push up
2500 newtons of force to push up
50 newtons of force to push up

quiz_questions.explanation: Ideally (no friction), work in = work out. Force x distance = weight x height. F x 5 = 500 x 1. F = 100 N. The ramp reduces the force from 500 N to 100 N by increasing the distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A single fixed pulley is used to lift a 200 N weight. How much force must you pull with (ignoring friction)?

options.text: 200 N of force on the rope end
100 N of force on the rope end
400 N of force on the rope end
50 N of force on the rope end

quiz_questions.explanation: A single fixed pulley only changes the direction of force, not the amount. You still need to pull with 200 N, but you can pull downward instead of lifting upward, which is more convenient.

quiz_questions.question_text: A crowbar is used to pry open a crate. What type of simple machine is being used?

options.text: A pulley to redirect the force
An inclined plane for sliding up
A wedge for splitting materials apart
A lever to multiply the applied force

quiz_questions.explanation: A crowbar is a lever. The edge of the crate acts as the fulcrum. By pushing down on the long end, you multiply the force at the short end, prying the crate open with much more force than you apply.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is it easier to push a heavy box up a long, gentle ramp than a short, steep one?

options.text: The longer ramp requires less force over a greater distance
The longer ramp reduces the weight of the heavy box overall
The shorter ramp actually requires less total force to push
The longer ramp eliminates friction between box and surface

quiz_questions.explanation: A longer, gentler ramp has a greater mechanical advantage. You apply less force over a longer distance. The total work is the same, but the reduced force makes the task manageable.

quiz_questions.question_text: A flagpole uses a pulley system. What advantage does this provide?

options.text: You can raise the flag by pulling the rope downward
The flag becomes lighter when attached to the pulley
The pulley creates energy to lift the flag upward
The pulley eliminates gravity on the flag completely

quiz_questions.explanation: The flagpole pulley changes the direction of force. Instead of climbing up to push the flag up, you pull the rope down from the ground, and the pulley redirects this force upward to raise the flag.

quiz_questions.question_text: A jar lid is hard to open. What simple machine principle makes it work?

options.text: The lid works as a screw: an inclined plane wrapped in a spiral
The lid works as a lever: a bar rotating around a fulcrum point
The lid works as a pulley: a wheel redirecting the applied force
The lid works as a wedge: splitting the jar from the lid top

quiz_questions.explanation: A jar lid has spiral threads, making it a screw. The threads are an inclined plane wrapped around the lid. Turning the lid converts rotational motion into linear motion, sealing or opening the jar.

quiz_questions.question_text: An axe splits a log. What two simple machines are combined in the axe?

options.text: A pulley attached to an inclined plane
A wedge (blade) on a lever (handle)
A screw connected to a wheel and axle
Two inclined planes and one pulley system

quiz_questions.explanation: An axe combines a wedge (the V-shaped blade that splits wood) with a lever (the handle that multiplies force). Many tools are compound machines combining multiple simple machines.

quiz_questions.question_text: A wheelchair ramp at a building entrance is 10 m long and rises 0.5 m. What is the ideal mechanical advantage?

options.text: 5 times force multiplication
10 times force multiplication
20 times force multiplication
0.05 times force multiplication

quiz_questions.explanation: For an inclined plane, ideal mechanical advantage = length / height = 10 m / 0.5 m = 20. The ramp multiplies force by 20, meaning you need only 1/20 of the weight to push up the ramp.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person uses a screwdriver to tighten a screw. What simple machine principles are at work?

options.text: Only the screw principle is at work here
Wheel and axle (handle) and screw (threads) work together
Only the lever principle is at work in this case
Only the wedge principle is at work for this task

quiz_questions.explanation: A screwdriver combines wheel and axle (the handle is the wheel, the shaft is the axle) with a screw (the fastener's threads). The handle multiplies force, and the screw threads convert rotation to linear clamping force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A construction worker uses a block and tackle (multiple pulleys) to lift a 1000 N beam. With 4 supporting rope sections, what force is needed?

options.text: 250 N of force to lift the beam up
1000 N of force to lift the beam up
4000 N of force to lift the beam up
500 N of force to lift the beam up

quiz_questions.explanation: With 4 supporting rope sections, the mechanical advantage is 4. Force = load / MA = 1000 N / 4 = 250 N. You pull with only 250 N, but you must pull 4 times as much rope.
